speaker: interviewer
text: It's so meaningful, you know. Let me know when you are not working on a Friday

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Then I'll make this. If you're not working on a Friday at 11:30. Anytime that you don't work, give me a call.

speaker: interviewer
text: October the 10, October the 29, '94 Drotow, Al, Rothschild, Mary, West Hollywood, LA, English. And Peter on the camera on the first take.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is October 29, 1994. I'm interviewing Al Drotow. My name is Mary Rothschild for the Survivors of Shoah Foundation. Please tell us your name before the war, the date of your birth, the place of your birth, and a little bit about your family life before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name before the war was Abraham Abe Drotovnic. I had two sisters. And I was born in Lithuania, the city of Kaunas or Kovno. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had four brothers, four sisters. None of them survived. My father had a brother in Kovno. And did not survive either. OK, now what else?

speaker: interviewer
text: When was your birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birthday is December 16, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your family life before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Average. We weren't rich. We weren't poor. My father was a chemist. Made more or less a decent living. And my mother was always a housewife. Both my sisters were in school. My older sister was in gymnasium. What else can I tell you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a close-knit family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you become aware that there was something wrong?

speaker: interviewee
text: You're talking about the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, 1941, when they-- we know about it before that something was going on. But 1941, when they attacked Russia, Lithuania got attacked first. We were occupied by the Russians since 1940. And in 1941, on June 22, we were attacked by, by the Nazis. And the ghetto was created in 1941 in around, it was June, July, in August, I believe. And I was about 15 years old. It was-- after a while it was almost starvation. There was no food of any kind. And I used to go out to the fence to bring some food to the house. I was shot several times on it. I was arrested twice. I was beaten almost to death. And I still survived, and I came back. The Gestapo was looking for me because I hit a German when I survived the second time. And I was hiding out. And until I went through three different roundups. Now, the first one, my father disappeared, my younger uncle with two children. The second one was in 1942, I believe, someplace around November or October, when there were about 15,000 people shot on the Ninth Fort, what they call. And we survived that one. Later on, we were shipped out to Riga. That was in 1942, someplace in October or November, the end of year. We were three night-- four nights and three days in the, in the train. That wasn't a train for people. It was a train for cattle, cattle train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was with you, Al?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, my two sisters and some other people that they caught for work. Now, I have to make it clear that I was-- only the last camp, Dachau was a concentration camp. The rest of the camps I worked was what they call in German Arbeitslager. Means working camps. Riga was also a working camp. They fed us not too bad. We had enough potatoes and some beans. And you worked. And we were fed so we would survive. [INAUDIBLE] Lagerfuhrer said in those places, if you has people, they have to work, you have to feed them. Otherwise, a dead Jew is no good to anybody. And [SIGHS] and then we went to-- back to Lithuania. We went to Stutthof. We were there a couple weeks. From Stutthof, to Panevezys, from Panevezys went to Dachau. And that was the worst. I almost did not survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's see, 1944 and 1945, about a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you arrive there with any members of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got separated in Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the rest of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened to who?

speaker: interviewer
text: To the rest of your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, what happened is my-- my mommy and my two sisters were in a camp in Poland. I think it was also Arbeitslager, working camp. And they survived the war. And I didn't see them. I was liberated in 1945. Now when would [INAUDIBLE] in April or May-- April, 28. And I didn't know about them till about 1946, possible seven, eight months later, after the liberation. And somebody came from-- somebody that I knew saw my sisters and my mother in Berlin. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And then they went to Frankfurt am Main, and I joined them later on. And we  stayed until 1948. And the went to Israel in 1948. And I decided to stay a little longer in Germany. And then the papers were made out to go to America. I decided to give it a try. It was 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your arrival in Dachau and your life in Dachau, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dachau was terrible. Dachau was starvation camps. They worked you. And they didn't give you to eat. And I went down to a weight that I almost couldn't work.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was saved by a-- by a man that-- what was his name? He was a-- he was a kapo. Somebody told him it's not, not important, that there is a Kovno [YIDDISH]. What they say in Yiddish, a-- a young man from Kovno that is almost finished. And would he can do something. So he did.

speaker: interviewee
text: He transferred me to the [GERMAN]. If you know what it is, this is where they do mechanical work. Since I was an old pupil, and I knew how to operate machines, they put me at night. And that put me back on my feet so I could start walking again. But still things were worse later on in the end of 1945. I was down on the march. I was down to almost-- I was finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now the interesting story that I have to tell you, that I was saved by an old German. We were laying in the woods, and it was just about April 26, or 25, or whatever, just a couple of days before we were liberated. A motorcycle with a beiwagen-- you know what a beiwagen is? The one that has a thing on it. Two Germans had a machine gun on it. And he asked the old man, was about 80 years old, the old German was guarding us. And he asked him in German, [GERMAN]. You understand German? What has-- what kind of-- what kind of-- what do have-- what are you going to do with the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the old German said, I have [GERMAN]. It means, I have to bring him to Roll, which was the upper, I think, of Bavaria. And the two young Germans, SS people, said, no, [GERMAN]. You cannot do it any more. It's too [GERMAN]. It's too late.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the, the young German said, [GERMAN]. Give me-- get out of the way. I'll shoot them all. So the old German, 80-year-old, had an automatic. He said, [GERMAN]. What it means, I shoot first. So they went away. So we were saved by an old German. I was-- he would have shot everybody. There was about 300 people laying there. Most of them could hardly move. And this was about, I would say, 35 kilometers before Wolfrathausen. This is a little city in Bavaria.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, we were liberated. And I was in, in Wolfrathausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Al, I'm going to stop you there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in Dachau for a year. Right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any specific incidents? Can you talk about miracles, times when you were saved?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- they were hanging people for bringing in a few potatoes that they caught on them. They were beating people to death. Then there was the quarantine. There was a time where everybody had typhus, quarantine they call  it, I believe. Everybody had typhus. And there were 12,000 people at the camp. There was 150 that were able to-- to-- healthy, like I was. I never had no typhus. Never had no sicknesses. And they were beating people every day. And everybody that got sick, they put them in some kind of a wagon and they pulled them away. And was lager five. That went there, number five. They pulled them over there and they had a crematorium in there. And they burned them in there. Or they took them to Dachau. I don't know exactly. I wasn't sure. But lager five was-- [GERMAN] what they call it-- a lager that they had sick people in. They never survived in there. I survived a lot of things that weren't-- we were working, we were walking to work, starting about 5:00 in the morning and bringing us back 10:00, 11:00 at night. And there was a ditch. And there was water in it. And people were pushing. And I fell in that thing. It was in the winter time, in 1945. It was cold. It probably was 10 below zero or something like that. It was froze all up. And they took me back to the barrack. And I was all-- and in the morning I went to work, never got sick. Well, the end is that I survived, and then I went to this country. And I was New Jersey. I was in New York. And then I want to see my-- in 1940-- in 1954-- '55, I went to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after you came out of Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very sick, very sick. I was three months in the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Wolfrathausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had diarrhea. And I could hardly walk. But I survived. Three months later I got-- first I got heavy. And then I went down to what I'm-- approximately what I am now. And I was very, very sick, stomach, and you name it. But-- but-- so, wife, do you want any more questions? I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after you got out of the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to work for the Americans in the kitchen. And, like I said, I got-- I was eating a lot. I got very heavy and then went down to normal. And then, I find out about my-- my mother and my sister. They were in-- in Frankfurt am Main. They were in a camp called Hoechst. And I went over there. And I remember traveling on the top of the roof on the trains because there was no room inside. I traveled all night. It was about 300 kilometers. And I stayed in Frankfurt am Main in Hoechst until 1949. My family left before me, 1948. 1949, I came over here. Came first to Columbus, Ohio. I had a friend in Trenton, New Jersey. And then, of course, I came over-- over here to Los Angeles. That was 1952. Stayed here until 1955. And then I went to Israel to visit my, my mother and my-- my sisters. And this is how I met Bracha. This is her Hebrew name. We got married and that's it. And we came back here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we have two sons, Harry and Bobby.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- what would you like your children to learn from you experiences in concentration camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my son, he knows a lot more than I in many ways. And he knows from me a little bit, what I told him. We always told our children what happened to us and how we survived and all that. Some people don't ever tell anything to their children, but we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he knows almost everything. I told him how, and where, and how we survived. [SIGHS] What else can I tell you? What would you like the world to know from your experiences? What have you learned?

speaker: interviewee
text: That we should forgive but not forget, never forget. Never forget what happened. Like I was telling that young lady there before, there was a German across the street. He was 30 years old. He asked me if I hate him. I said no. How can I hate you for it? You weren't even there. You weren't born yet. How can you be responsible for people before you that you weren't even alive? You weren't-- you weren't there, so you couldn't have done no harm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we should, we should forgive, but not forget, never forget what happened. We see what happening right now. There are some people that they never change, the same way. Like the Ukrainians, they never changed. That's what Wiesenthal said. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's still take one. Second take.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, let's go back to 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Kaunas ghetto. I came out to the fence to get some food. Came back at night with a package. And the guard was [INAUDIBLE]. He was shouting halt! Halt! Halt! And I didn't halt. Finally, I fell in a well. The hole down in there, I was hanging on for a long time. I don't know for how long. I couldn't get out because the bullets were coming all over. I guess it was, it was around midnight. And by the time it's quieted down and I could crawl out, it was morning. So that was quite an experience and it was very, very cold. OK, the second thing I'll tell you is not-- I didn't beat a German. I, I, I hit a German once when I, I told you that before, that when survived from the Gestapo. They were looking for me. But what happened was in Riga. And It was around 1943. There was a guy and he was Jewish. He was an actor. He was also a barber. He was privileged because he was cutting the hair of the Nazis, the Germans. And he was privileged. They kept him in the camp. And he had better, better things than other people, better food, better clothes. And my mother was coming up. The men were separated from the women. You were in a separate block what they call. And my mother was coming up. She was-- she wouldn't eat herself, leaving a little bit more for the children, because she says she doesn't have to eat so much. And we were young.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this guy was always bothering her. He was a man in his thirties, a young man. And I was about 16 or something. And once that got to me. And I beat him up real bad. And he was all swollen, his eyes and everything. Why did you beat him up?

speaker: interviewee
text: He bothered my mother. And the irony is that I thought they were going to beat me, or shoot me, or whatever. And the German called me in, and his name was Sumaher. He was a German. He was from the Wehrmacht, was a surgeon.

speaker: interviewee
text: He called me in. And he says, Drotovnic, what happened? Why did you beat, Charles was his name? [INAUDIBLE]. So I told him. Told him the true story. I said, this guy was beating my-- was bothering my mother. And he looked at me, and he said, for your mother, you should do everything. We're  German. Heil Hitler. So that's-- that's the whole story. I forgot about it. OK, can we go forward in time to Dachau? And can you describe if any day stands up in your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, I tell you, the hunger was so bad that people ate everything. And some people ate some stuff that weren't edible. And they got crazy from it. There were little things, they looked like blueberries. But they were red. And they ate that, and they got crazy from it. Some people-- you know, the fence was energized with power. Some people were so crazy, they went to the fence, and they got electrocuted. Like I told you before, some people got shot because they had a few potatoes in their pockets. I saw all kind of things. In the barracks, we were-- was made, there was a hole in the ground. And there was a little roof over the top that looked like-- oh, how can-- this way. So they make the kind of roof that the water will run off good. And we were, about, maybe 50 people in one of them things. And it was bad. It was very bad. People had lice. And then [INAUDIBLE] come. And they had people beaten to death right over there. And they couldn't, they couldn't go fast enough, run out fast enough. [SIGHS] Well, and I was trying to forget all this, you know. But I cannot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any incidents that stand up in your mind from the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, every day was just-- you know, they, they shoot people all the time. People trying to escape, people not-- people hiding out from the work, people not-- not getting up in time. Life had no meaning for the Nazis. No, just, absolutely none.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any times when you could have died and a miracle saved you when you were in--

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you about the miracle. That was it. If that old German would be a bastard, he would-- he would have said, hey, go ahead and do it. But apparently they weren't all that bad. Among 80 million Germans, there must have been some people that didn't believe in Hitler's theory. So he was one of them. And he was my-- our angel, my angel. And see why I'm, why I'm here today. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Take three.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Well, I've told you before, what one other thing. I already knew in 1942, that the war is lost for the Germans. I worked with Germans on the [GERMAN] was what they call. And there-- and they weren't all bastards. Some of them were-- they were civilians. They were not, not military people. And one German, Albert, told me-- he showed me the paper. They didn't tell everything in the paper. But he read between the lines. And Stalingrad was gone, and he was talking about Paulus, General Paulus. That the war, they cannot win any more. But it will take a long time. OK, the thing I'm did forget to say is I wanted to live to see the end, the end. I didn't care if after that I die. But the end, I wanted to see the end. And it happens, I saw the end. And I saw the Germans, that they were so-- they, they were so down after the war. They were suffering. They didn't have no food. And they were coming-- Germans were working for people that were Jews to clean up their houses to get a piece of bread or to eat something. So that made me feel better, that I saw the end. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Al, was there any time when you were in Dachau when things were-- horrible things were happening to you? When you told yourself, I wish I could live so I can tell the story?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw the horrible things, and I said to myself, Abe, you have to live through it. You have to see the end. I saw things that-- where people were beaten to death, killed to death, hung, hung for three potatoes. And I said, I'm going to stay away from these things and I'm going to live through it. And I did. I wanted to see the end. That was my main goal, is to see the end and what's going to happen. [SIGHS] Well, I don't think I have anything more, I don't, to tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is my grandfather, my father's father. I don't know where this picture was taken. I don't know when. We found it in New York. My-- my uncle had it, my-- my father's brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Drotovnic. And his first name-- first name, what was his first name? I don't-- I don't know. I don't remember. I couldn't tell you. I don't remember. Yes, this is my mother. And her name before she was married was Fania Klas. And she died a few years ago in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture could have been taken in the United States--

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe in the 1970s or something like that, yeah. That's my father, I don't know when that picture was taken. I couldn't tell you. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Tuwa Drotovnic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Poland? Lithuania?

speaker: interviewee
text: Could have been taken in Poland. No, not Poland. Lithuania, yeah, Lithuania, most likely, yeah, most likely Lithuania. Yes, this is my sister and myself. This was probably taken in 1926. I probably here one year old or she must be three years old here. This was also found-- my Uncle Max had it in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Haia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is she in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is she in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: She is on, on the left. And I'm on my right. Yes, this was taken in-- this is a picture of the whole family. It was taken in Frankfurt am Main, Hoechst. This is my brother-in-law and my younger sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: My younger sister is to the left. My brother-in-law to the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: This Simon and Ida. And my sister Haia is to the left. My mother is in the center, and myself to the right. It was taken around 1950. It was taken 1947 probably. Yeah, around 1947, yeah. Yes, this is our wedding picture. Was taken in 1955. My former girlfriend to the left, and I am to the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bracha Koenig was her name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Israel, in Tiv'on. Tiv'on, Israel, yeah, that's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is a picture of my wife, my son, and myself. Was taken in 1950-- '68, Barbara, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: BARBARA DOTROW (WIFE): '58. '58, yeah? 1958? Yeah, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken right here in Los Angeles, no? BARBARA DOTROW (WIFE): No, in New York. In New York, I'm sorry. OK, this is a picture of my wife, Barbara Drotow; myself, Al Drotow; and my two sons. My oldest son Harry is to the right. And my youngest son Bobby, to the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken, Los Angeles, and the year-- do you know the year, Barbara? BARBARA DOTROW (WIFE): 1964. 1950-- '64, yeah.
